centlemen:
I respectfully request of the Fair Political 
Practices
Cornmission under the Po]iticaf Reform Act of 1974 and as perrnitted

1. 
Does my occupation as a real estate broker who o$/ns and
nanages a loca1 real estate firm in the Yuba city area and at the
sarne time serving as a nenber of the Yuba city Planning Connission
in and of itself 
and without regard to specific projects,
applications, rezonings, permit approvals, subdivision approvals
and the like, namefy in a vacuum, constitute a conflict of interest
which woul-d effectively preclude me from serving on the Commission
on a theory that there is somehow some inherent incompatibility or
conflict betr,,reen servi.ng on a local planning conunission and at the
same time owning and being the managing broker of an active locat
real estate firm.
2. Assurning that after I have voted on a particular planning
issue in which I was satisfied that I had no economic interest,
either direct or indirect, at the tirne the decision was made in
that particular 
planning, zoning or subdivision matter and
parenthetically one in which I neither anticj.pated, suspected, was
promised or assured of any future financial source of income and
subsequently either one of ny real estate agents and/or myself for
that matter had occasion to represent a buyer in the nultipte
listings service cooperative sale on the pioject that was the
subject of the prior approvar and/or somehovr subsequently becarne
invoLved in a subsequent listing of that property oi sonj portion
thereof for sale or for resale, would that somehow taint m| prior
participation in that planning decision so as to now const-itute a
confrict of interest which wourd somehow preclude me or prohibit rne
fron participating in the listing of that property o. i.h" sale of
that property under the muftiple listing service.-


Page 2
To give you sone brief background which gives rise to the
forgoing request for opinion, I am currentfy the owner and managing
broker of $/hat I like to believe is an active l-ocal- real estate
firn in the Yuba City area with offices at 1435 Butte House Road
which business is a member of the local- multiple Iisting service.
I have orrrned and nanaged that business now for several years. I
currently serve on the Yuba City Planning Conmission as a nember of
that commission and have no$/ for several years,
Differences of opinion exist between a nember of the Yuba City
Council and nyself v/ith reference to specif ica1.'Ly srhat does
constitute a conflict 
of interest within the meaning of the
Political Reform Act of 1,974. Shortly after the last election that
council mernber asked for ny resignation. 
That council person
charged that was I'there is no way that you can be a Planning
Conmissi,oner vrhile being a real estate broker without there being
a conffict of interestr'. 
That council persons position was
essentially that as a member of the Cornnission voting on zoning,
subdivision and other issues which necessarily impacted to some
extent growth in the comnunity and as a rnember of the real estate
profession I somehorr/ had some inherent conflict or some inherent
incornpatibil ity .
That charge prompted me to seek counsel with the contract City
Attorney regarding this natter and he revier,red with rne, at some
length, the various conffict of interest statutes and specifically
the provisions of covernment Code Section 87100 et seq as to what
constitutes a conflict of interest and as to hrhat speci.fically
constitutes a material financial- interest in a decision that s/ou1d
come before the Conrnission. As a result of such discussion there
was an instance where, based upon the City Attorneyrs advice, a
matter was reagendized with myself abstaining fron any vote on the
issue because of a potential conflict of interest and the record
was so clarified and that notential, confl-ict of interest placed on
the record.
I have been extremely sensitive to the conflict of j-nterest
issue since that tine and have in any case where I felt such a
conflict existed abstained and foregone any decision making and/or
participation in the decision makj.ng process.
Being particularly sensitive to the issue and recognizing that
ny voting record on matters that cone before the planning
Commission are now being closely rnonitored, it is important for rny
own piece of rnind to have sone clarificatj.on at Leas! in a general
sense so that this issue does not sonehow get out of hand and be
blown totally out of proportion.


Page 3
What I have specifically 
in mind is that as you can
appreciate, various types of projects come before the Commission
for approval from time to tirne. 
some of those projects are
subdivision approval- projects at the tentative map stage, general
plan amendments, rezoning projects, permit applications, plan
devel-opment approvalsr variance applications and the like. 
I am
quick to recognize that there are obvious direct and/or indirect
economic interests which $rould preclude my participation 
in
individual projects. 
Cl,early property in which I r,/ould have an
interest or in which I would have a leasehold interest or in which
I would have sone financial interest in the business entity that
woul,d be seeking to devel-op the property, the subject of the
applicatj.on, or in which for exanple I have had business dealings
or ongoing business dealings with developers of a particular
project which would constitute a source of income to rne within the
meaning of the conflict of interest statutes. 
Those instances
obviously I woul,d not participate in nor would f attenpt to
influence the decision making process. 
There are, however,
situations that I can foresee where I have voted on a particular
planning issue in which I had no econornic i.nterest, either direct
or indirect, at the time the decision rnaking process was involved
nor in which I anticipated, suspected or foresaw any future
econornic interest nor one in which I was promised or assured of any
future econornic interest and then subsequently ej,ther myself or one
of rny agents had an opportunity to either represent a buyer in the
rnultiple Iistings service, which is a cooperative service in
connection with a sale on a piece of property that was the subject
of that prior developnent, or for that matter to take a subsequent
Iisting of that property.
What concerns me now is that somehow sornebody will argue that
my subsequent contact l4rith that property or some portion of that
property that was the subject of a prior approval- in which I
participated at the time I had no direct or indirect economic
j.nterest is now going to be construed as sone sort of a conflict
and will sonehow be clained to taint my prior participation in the
planning process on that project and that this woutd now sornehow
precl-ude me from either listing that property for sale or resale or
some portion thereof or representing a buyer in the multiple
listings service j.n connection with that property or some portion
thereof.
I would like to have the issue clarified for me from the Fair
Political Practices Commission,s standpoint and at the same time
woul"d like to have the general question ansr./ered on which a prior
charge has been leveLed that somehow real estate brokers and/or
owners and/ or managers of real estate firms are sonehow inherently
precluded fron serving their cornnunity as a nember of a planning
Comrnission body on the theory that there j-s somehow some inhereni
conflict of interest between the two (2) positions.

Your early
that hopefully
issue.
response to
the air canthese questions
be cLeared in

yours ,

sections.question:
1. Does my occupation as a real estate broker who owns and manages a local real estate firm in the Yuba City area and at the same time serving as a member of the Yuba City Planning Commission in and of itself and without regard to specific projects, applications, rezonings, permit approvals, subdivision approvals and the like, namely in a vacuum, constitute a conflict of interest which would effectively preclude me from serving on the Commission on a theory that there is somehow some inherent incompatibility or conflict between serving on a local planning commission and at the same time owning and being the managing broker of an active local real estate firm.

2. Assuming that after I have voted on a particular planning issue in which I was satisfied that I had no economic interest, either direct or indirect, at the time the decision was made in that particular planning, zoning or subdivision matter and parenthetically one in which I neither anticipated, suspected, was promised or assured of any future financial source of income and subsequently either one of my real estate agents and/or myself for that matter had occasion to represent a buyer in the multiple listings service cooperative sale on the project that was the subject of the prior approval and/or somehow subsequently became involved in a subsequent listing of that property or some portion thereof for sale or for resale, would that somehow taint my prior participation in that planning decision so as to now constitute a conflict of interest which would somehow preclude me or prohibit me from participating in the listing of that property or the sale of that property under the multiple listing service.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a real estate broker who owns and manages a local real estate firm serve on a planning commission without an inherent conflict of interest, and does subsequent real estate activity on properties previously voted on create a retroactive conflict of interest?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or short answer section present. Document dated October 21, 1991. Requestor seeks clarification on Political Reform Act provisions regarding conflicts of interest and the compatibility of serving as both a planning commissioner and real estate broker.

Does my occupation as a real estate broker who owns and manages a local real estate firm in the Yuba City area and at the same time serving as a member of the Yuba City Planning Commission in and of itself and without regard to specific projects, applications, rezonings, permit approvals, subdivision approvals and the like, namely in a vacuum, constitute a conflict of interest which would effectively preclude me from serving on the Commission on a theory that there is somehow some inherent incompatibility or conflict between serving on a local planning commission and at the same time owning and being the managing broker of an active local real estate firm.

2. Assuming that after I have voted on a particular planning issue in which I was satisfied that I had no economic interest, either direct or indirect, at the time the decision was made in that particular planning, zoning or subdivision matter and parenthetically one in which I neither anticipated, suspected, was promised or assured of any future financial source of income and subsequently either one of my real estate agents and/or myself for that matter had occasion to represent a buyer in the multiple listings service cooperative sale on the project that was the subject of the prior approval and/or somehow subsequently became involved in a subsequent listing of that property or some portion thereof for sale or for resale, would that somehow taint my prior participation in that planning decision so as to now constitute a conflict of interest which would somehow preclude me or prohibit me from participating in the listing of that property or the sale of that property under the multiple listing service.

embedding.summary: Lee E. Welch, a real estate broker and Yuba City Planning Commission member, requests an FPPC advisory opinion on whether his dual roles constitute an inherent conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act of 1974, and whether subsequent real estate transactions involving properties he previously voted on would taint his prior participation.
